topic: Someone finds their parent's draft letter of resignation from decades ago
raw_output:
[story 1]

The brittle, yellowed paper felt alien in my hands, a relic unearthed from the dusty confines of an old filing cabinet. It was my father’s handwriting, elegant and precise, yet the words chilled me to the bone. A draft resignation letter, dated from nineteen-seventy-eight. I unfolded it slowly, my breath catching in my throat. The language spoke of disillusionment, of a profound sense of being undervalued, and the casual dismissal of a lifetime of dedication. My jaw tightened, the muscles in my neck corded. I remember slamming the letter shut, the sound echoing too loudly in the quiet apartment. Why did he write something so damning, something so final, and then simply discard it? A wave of sharp, bitter resentment washed over me, hot and immediate.

[story 2]

I found the envelope tucked inside a forgotten box of old tax documents. The paper was thin, fragile, smelling faintly of aged tobacco and something sharper, like old regret. When I finally broke the seal, the script swam before my eyes. It was Dad’s hand, signing away his career, signing away years of hard work, all summarized in a few carefully chosen sentences. Reading it, a strange pressure built behind my sternum. My hands began to tremble, and I had to grip the edge of the desk until my knuckles were white. Every word felt like a deliberate slight, a calculated slight against the very foundation of who I thought he was. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small room, my footsteps heavy and uneven. The air felt thick, suffocating.

[story 3]

It was accidental. While clearing out the attic, I stumbled upon a sheaf of brittle correspondence. Among the faded photographs and moth-eaten linens, there it was: a letter. My father’s handwriting, stark black ink on aged cream paper. The content detailed his decision to step away from the firm, framed with a tone of utter contempt for the environment he was leaving. A sudden, intense heat rose in my cheeks. I stared at the page, my gaze refusing to move away, as if trying to burn the words into my retinas. A low, guttural sound escaped my throat—a sound of pure, unadulterated offense. I crumpled the paper once, twice, the sound of the tearing sound a small, violent release of the tension coiling in my chest.
